Root cause: The etcdoperator.v0.9.4 CSV was installed via a manually-approved InstallPlan but the operator pod failed to achieve minimum availability, causing OLM to mark the CSV as Failed and delete it, leaving the Subscription in UpgradePending state with no active CSV

dueDiligence.targetAccuracy: Targeting the etcd Subscription because it is the resource that controls the operator lifecycle. Remediating it (e.g., deleting and recreating, or triggering a new InstallPlan) will cause OLM to reinstall the CSV. The CSV itself is gone and cannot be targeted directly.
dueDiligence.proportionality: Targeting the Subscription is appropriate — it is the entry point for OLM to reinstall the failed CSV. The scope is limited to the demo-operator namespace etcd operator install. Other CSVs (logic-operator, gitops-operator) are in Succeeded state and not affected.
dueDiligence.scopeCompleteness: Investigated: etcd Subscription, InstallPlan install-5xnn9, OperatorGroup demo-operator-group, all CSVs in demo-operator, events in demo-operator, pod search for etcd-operator, logic-operator deployment in openshift-serverless-logic. Did not investigate: node metrics at time of failure, OLM operator logs, previous version etcdoperator.v0.9.2 state.
dueDiligence.causalCompleteness: Traced from OperatorCSVFailed signal → missing CSV → killed pod → OLM ComponentUnhealthy → pod startup failure. Cannot go deeper without pod logs (pod no longer exists) or node-level metrics at the time of failure. The exact reason for the pod kill (probe failure vs crash vs external) is unverifiable post-hoc.
dueDiligence.evidenceSufficiency: Claims backed by: (1) kubectl events showing pod kill at 02:12:44Z, (2) kubectl find showing no etcdoperator.v0.9.4 CSV exists, (3) Subscription status showing UpgradePending with installedCSV pointing to missing CSV, (4) InstallPlan showing Complete but CSV subsequently deleted. Pod logs are inaccessible (pod no longer exists). Node metrics at failure time are unverified.
dueDiligence.regressionAwareness: No prior remediation history available for this target per get_namespaced_resource_context output (regression_detected: false, no tier1/tier2 history).
dueDiligence.alternativeHypotheses: Alternative 1: Image pull failure — ruled out, events show containers were Created and Started before being killed. Alternative 2: RBAC/permission issue — ruled out, InstallPlan shows Role and RoleBinding created successfully. Alternative 3: OperatorGroup mismatch — ruled out, OperatorGroup targets demo-operator namespace matching singlenamespace-alpha channel. Alternative 4: CatalogSource unhealthy — ruled out, all 5 catalog sources are healthy per Subscription status.
dueDiligence.confidenceCalibration: Starting at 1.0. -0.10: Pod logs unavailable (pod deleted, cannot confirm exact failure reason). -0.05: Node metrics at failure time not verified (cannot confirm resource pressure was causal). -0.05: Cannot determine if the pod kill was OLM-initiated vs external deletion vs crash. Final confidence: 0.80.

contributingFactors: etcd Subscription uses Manual installPlanApproval, requiring explicit approval before install proceeds — the plan was approved ~5s after creation (scripted/automated), suggesting non-standard deployment workflow

The etcd operator pod started three containers (etcd-operator, etcd-backup-operator, etcd-restore-operator) which were all killed simultaneously at 02:12:44Z, ~28 seconds after starting — consistent with failed readiness probes, OLM health-check failure, or external deletion

The CSV etcdoperator.v0.9.4 is no longer present in the cluster (not found by search across all namespaces) — it was garbage-collected or deleted after the pod failure, leaving Subscription in UpgradePending state
OperatorGroup demo-operator-group targets only ['demo-operator'] namespace, matching the singlenamespace-alpha channel — OperatorGroup configuration is correct and not a contributing factor

The logic-operator-rhel8-controller-manager deployment experienced two separate ComponentUnhealthy failures (2026-05-05 and 2026-05-06) on the same node dev-worker-0, suggesting possible node-level resource pressure that could also affect the etcd operator pod startup
